statement.en: The Holographic Boundary Principle: A Geometric Normalization from Stellar Photospheres to the Cosmic Horizon
statement.summary: It is a operational reality of astrophysics that observers do not measure continuous 3D bulk volumes, but rather the 2D decoupling surfaces where radiation escapes. In this Letter, the operational requirement of radiative decoupling is formalized as a geometric boundary condition. By coupling measurable radiative observables with Newtonian surface gravity, a dimensionless boundary invariant, 𝒳, is defined. Evaluated analytically under standard macroscopic closures, 𝒳 reduces to an exact mass-independent identity. Observationally, using strictly independent solar measurements and a benchmark kinematic sample of 190 detached eclipsing binaries, stellar photospheres are found to lie below the theoretical blackbody phase-space capacity 𝒳₀ = π³/15 ≃ 2.0671. Projecting this strictly 2D surfac

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The paper presents a novel geometric formalism (boundary invariant 𝒳) derived from operational astrophysics. It claims analytical reduction to mass-independent identity and presents observational validation on stellar photospheres and eclipsing binaries. However: (1) preprint status (2026-04-25, arXiv categories suggest recent submission); (2) the abstract is truncated, preventing full evaluation; (3) the claim that 𝒳 'reduces to exact mass-independent identity' requires independent peer verification; (4) the projection to cosmic horizon is mentioned but not developed in visible text. The work exhibits mathematical rigor in formalization but requires community validation before assignment to TRUE or FALSE. Status: provisional, under validation.
